C of I Admission Data Analysis

Author

Rabin, Ashley, Prabin and Tadeo

Published

April 28, 2025

1 Introduction

1.1 Objective

The primary goal of this analysis is to build a predictive model to estimate whether a prospective student will enroll (Enrolled) based on their characteristics and interactions with the college. This can help the admissions office optimize recruitment strategies and resource allocation.

1.2 Data Source

The dataset consists of anonymized prospect data for the years 2022-2024, provided by the college’s admissions department. Note: If any individual identification is possible, Dr. Friedlander must be contacted immediately.

1.3 Data Description

Initial Prospect Data (FallData): 108,437 records. Filtered Prospect Data (FallData_filtered): 9,587 records (filtered based on application status). Final Dataset with Enrollment Status (Enrolled_FallData): 5,584 records.

The dataset contains records for prospective students. Key features include:

  • Prospect: Was information on this prospect purchased? (Y/N)
  • Inquiry: Did the student reach out to us first? (Y/N)
  • Applicant: Did the student apply? (Y/N)
  • Admit: Was the student admitted? (Y/N)
  • Conversion Rate: Did the student convert from an admit to a deposit? (Y/N)
  • Deposit: Did the student put down a deposit? (Y/N)
  • Yield Rate: Did the student convert from a deposit to an enrolled student? (Y/N)
  • Drops-DP/DF: Deposits who don’t enroll or defer.
  • Net Deposits: Deposits that matriculate that fall.
  • Sex: Sex of student.
  • Student Type: Freshman or Transfer.
  • IPEDS Classification: Race category used by IPEDS.
  • Geographic Region: Region where the student lives.
  • Active County: County if from Idaho.
  • First_Source Origin: Where the prospect data was initially obtained.
  • First Source Summary: Summary of the first source.
  • Application Source: How the student submitted their application.
  • Entry Term: Intended matriculation term.
  • School X Type: Type of school X on their application.
  • School X Institution: Name of school X on their application.
  • School X Verified GPA: GPA from school X.
  • ACT and SAT variables: Standardized test scores.
  • Sport X Sport: Sport interest.
  • Academic Interest: Stated academic interest.
  • Enrolled: Target variable created during data cleaning, indicating if the student ultimately enrolled (1 for enrolled, 0 for not enrolled).

The primary task involves cleaning, augmenting, and feature engineering this data to predict the Enrolled status based on predictors like Sex, Student Type, IPEDS Classification, Geographic Region, GPA, test scores, and academic/sport interests.

2 Data Preparation

2.1 Loading & Cleaning the Data

The data are loaded from Fall2022Data.csv, Fall2023Data.csv, Fall2024Data.csv. Unnecessary features such as thumbnail details, creation details, and others are removed to simplify the dataset.

Fall2022Data <- read.csv("Fall2022Data.csv")
Fall2023Data <- read.csv("Fall2023Data.csv")
Fall2024Data <- read.csv("Fall2024Data.csv")

Fall2022Data <- Fall2022Data |>
  mutate(origin = "Fall2022Data")

Fall2023Data <- Fall2023Data |>
  mutate(origin = "Fall2023Data")

Fall2024Data <- Fall2024Data |>
  mutate(origin = "Fall2024Data")

FallData <- bind_rows(Fall2022Data, Fall2023Data, Fall2024Data)

FallData <- FallData |>
  mutate(across(where(is.character), as.factor))


FallData$School..1.Verified.GPA <- ifelse(FallData$School..1.Verified.GPA > 5,
                                          FallData$School..1.Verified.GPA / 100 * 4,
                                          FallData$School..1.Verified.GPA)

FallData$School..2.Verified.GPA <- ifelse(FallData$School..2.Verified.GPA > 5,
                                          FallData$School..2.Verified.GPA / 100 * 4,
                                          FallData$School..2.Verified.GPA)

FallData$School..3.Verified.GPA <- ifelse(
  FallData$School..3.Verified.GPA == 4317,
  FallData$School..3.Verified.GPA / 1000,
  FallData$School..3.Verified.GPA)

FallData$School..3.Verified.GPA <- ifelse(FallData$School..3.Verified.GPA > 5,
                                          FallData$School..3.Verified.GPA / 100 * 4,
                                          FallData$School..3.Verified.GPA)
# Find the index of the Applicant column
applicant_idx <- which(names(FallData) == "Applicant")

# Filter rows where "Y" appears in Applicant or any column to the right
FallData_filtered <- FallData |>
  filter(apply(
    FallData[, applicant_idx:ncol(FallData)], 1,
    function(row) any(row == "Y")
  ))

admit_idx <- which(names(FallData_filtered) == "Yield.Rate")

Enrolled_FallData <- FallData_filtered |>
  mutate(
    Enrolled = apply(
      FallData_filtered, 1, function(row) {
        # Check if any "Y" in Yield Rate or right of Yield Rate
        any_right = any(row[(admit_idx):(ncol(FallData_filtered))] == "Y", na.rm = TRUE)
        # Check if any "Y" in left of Yield Rate
        any_left = any(row[1:(admit_idx-1)] == "Y", na.rm = TRUE)
        if (isTRUE(any_right)) {
          return(1)
        } else if (isTRUE(any_left)) {
          return(0)
        } else {
          return(NA)
        }
      }
    )
  )

head(Enrolled_FallData)

2.2 Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)

We calculate what percentage of total prospects make it to each stage in the admissions funnel: from Prospect → Applicant → Admit → Deposit → Enrolled. This provides a snapshot of how many students move forward at each stage and where the most significant drop-offs occur.

# Total number of students
total_students <- nrow(FallData)

# Funnel stage counts
funnel_counts <- c(
  Prospect = sum(FallData$Prospect == "Y", na.rm = TRUE),
  Applicant = nrow(FallData_filtered),
  Admit = sum(FallData$Admit == "Y", na.rm = TRUE),
  Deposit = sum(FallData$Deposit == "Y", na.rm = TRUE),
  Enrolled = sum(Enrolled_FallData$Enrolled == 1, na.rm = TRUE)
)

# Funnel stage percentages
funnel_percentages <- round((funnel_counts / total_students) * 100, 2)

# summary table
funnel_summary <- data.frame(
  Count = funnel_counts,
  Percentage = paste0(funnel_percentages, "%")
)

kable(funnel_summary, caption = "Funnel Stage Conversion Rates (Counts & %)", align = "c")
Funnel Stage Conversion Rates (Counts & %)
Count Percentage
Prospect 52650 48.55%
Applicant 9587 8.84%
Admit 6375 5.88%
Deposit 1085 1%
Enrolled 5584 5.15%

The funnel stage analysis revealed significant drop-offs at each step of the admissions process. Out of 108,437 total records, only 48.55% were identified as prospects, and just 8.84% applied. From there, 5.88% were admitted, 1.00% deposited, and 5.15% ultimately enrolled. The largest drop occurred between prospects and applicants, followed by admitted students who did not deposit. Interestingly, more students were marked as enrolled than deposited, suggesting either data inconsistencies or alternative enrollment pathways. These patterns highlight key areas for improving outreach, engagement, and data tracking across the admissions funnel.

We then created an Enrolled variable by scanning for a “Y” response in the Yield Rate column or beyond. This helped us classify students who ultimately enrolled.

# Finding the index of the Yield Rate column
admit_idx <- which(names(FallData) == "Yield.Rate")

# Created Enrolled column
FallData <- FallData |> 
  mutate(
    Enrolled = apply(FallData, 1, function(row) {
      any_right = any(row[(admit_idx):(ncol(FallData))] == "Y", na.rm = TRUE)
      any_left = any(row[1:(admit_idx - 1)] == "Y", na.rm = TRUE)
      if (isTRUE(any_right)) {
        return(1)
      } else if (isTRUE(any_left)) {
        return(0)
      } else {
        return(NA)
      }
    })
  )

2.2.1 Enrollment by Sex

We examined how enrollment rates vary between male and female students. Female students had a slightly higher enrollment rate than males, while students with unknown sex had a significantly lower enrollment rate.

FallData |> 
  mutate(Enrolled = factor(Enrolled)) |> 
  group_by(Sex, Enrolled) |> 
  summarise(count = n()) |> 
  pivot_wider(names_from = Enrolled, values_from = count, values_fill = 0) |> 
  mutate(total = `0` + `1`, enrolled_percent = round(`1` / total * 100, 2))
ggplot(FallData, aes(x = Sex, fill = factor(Enrolled))) +
  geom_bar(position = "fill") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  labs(title = "Enrollment Rate by Sex", y = "Percentage", fill = "Enrolled") +
  theme_minimal()

2.2.2 Enrollment by Student Type

We compared enrollment between Freshman and Transfer students. Although the majority of prospects were Freshmen, Transfer students enrolled at a much higher rate (over 56%).

FallData |> 
  mutate(Enrolled = factor(Enrolled)) |> 
  group_by(`Student.Type`, Enrolled) |> 
  summarise(count = n()) |> 
  pivot_wider(names_from = Enrolled, values_from = count, values_fill = 0) |> 
  mutate(total = `0` + `1`, enrolled_percent = round(`1` / total * 100, 2))
ggplot(FallData, aes(x = Student.Type, fill = factor(Enrolled))) +
  geom_bar(position = "fill") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  labs(title = "Enrollment Rate by Student Type", y = "Percentage", fill = "Enrolled") +
  theme_minimal()

2.2.3 Enrollment by Sport Interest

We analyzed how primary sport interest impacted enrollment. After grouping and ranking the data, we plotted the top 10 sports by number of enrolled students. Some sports had enrollment rates as high as 35–38%, making them promising recruitment areas.

FallData |> 
  mutate(Enrolled = factor(Enrolled)) |> 
  group_by(`Sport.1.Sport`, Enrolled) |> 
  summarise(count = n()) |> 
  pivot_wider(names_from = Enrolled, values_from = count, values_fill = 0) |> 
  mutate(total = `0` + `1`, enrolled_percent = round(`1` / total * 100, 2)) |> 
  arrange(desc(`1`))
sport_plot_data <- FallData |> 
  mutate(Enrolled = factor(Enrolled)) |> 
  group_by(`Sport.1.Sport`, Enrolled) |> 
  summarise(count = n(), .groups = "drop") |> 
  pivot_wider(names_from = Enrolled, values_from = count, values_fill = 0) |> 
  mutate(
    total = `0` + `1`,
    enrolled_percent = round(`1` / total * 100, 2)
  ) |> 
  arrange(desc(`1`)) |> 
  filter(!is.na(`Sport.1.Sport`)) |> 
  slice_max(`1`, n = 10)

# Plot
ggplot(sport_plot_data, aes(x = reorder(`Sport.1.Sport`, enrolled_percent), y = enrolled_percent)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#0073C2FF") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Enrollment Rate by Sport Interest (Top 10)",
    x = "Sport Interest",
    y = "Enrollment Rate (%)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

2.2.4 Enrollment by Academic Interest

We evaluated how enrollment varied by academic interest. Majors such as Exercise Science, Digital Marketing, and Undecided had notably high enrollment rates. This analysis can help target programs with strong follow-through.

FallData |> 
  mutate(Enrolled = factor(Enrolled)) |> 
  group_by(`Academic.Interest`, Enrolled) |> 
  summarise(count = n()) |> 
  pivot_wider(names_from = Enrolled, values_from = count, values_fill = 0) |> 
  mutate(total = `0` + `1`, enrolled_percent = round(`1` / total * 100, 2)) |> 
  arrange(desc(`1`))
FallData |> 
  filter(!is.na(Academic.Interest)) |> 
  count(Academic.Interest, Enrolled) |> 
  group_by(Academic.Interest) |> 
  mutate(prop = n / sum(n)) |> 
  ungroup() |> 
  group_by(Academic.Interest) |> 
  mutate(total = sum(n)) |> 
  filter(total > 100) |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(Academic.Interest, -prop), y = prop, fill = factor(Enrolled))) +
  geom_col(position = "fill") +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  labs(title = "Enrollment Rate by Academic Interest (Top Majors)", y = "Enrollment %", x = NULL, fill = "Enrolled") +
  theme_minimal()

We investigated the initial lead source for each student using First_Source.Origin.First.Source.Summary. This revealed which outreach methods or partnerships were most effective.

Top lead sources included:

  • Royal Search Responder

  • College Board PSAT, AP, SAT

  • Encoura (ACT)

  • Apply Idaho

We calculated and visualized the enrollment rate for each source (with at least 50 students), revealing which first sources had the highest yield.

FallData |> 
  count(`First_Source.Origin.First.Source.Summary`, sort = TRUE) |> 
  head(10)
FallData |> 
  filter(!is.na(`First_Source.Origin.First.Source.Summary`)) |> 
  group_by(`First_Source.Origin.First.Source.Summary`) |> 
  summarise(
    total = n(),
    enrolled = sum(Enrolled == 1, na.rm = TRUE),
    enrolled_rate = round(enrolled / total * 100, 2)
  ) |> 
  filter(total >= 50) |> 
  arrange(desc(enrolled_rate)) |> 
  slice_max(enrolled, n = 10) |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(`First_Source.Origin.First.Source.Summary`, enrolled_rate), y = enrolled_rate)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#0073C2FF") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Enrollment Rate by First Source (Top 10)", y = "Enrollment Rate (%)", x = "First Source") +
  theme_minimal()

2.2.5 Enrollment Distribution

Visualizing the distribution of the Enrolled variable:

# Code to visualize the distribution of the 'Enrolled' variable
# Example:
# ggplot(Enrolled_FallData, aes(x = factor(Enrolled))) +
#  geom_bar() +
#  labs(title = "Distribution of Enrollment Status", x = "Enrolled (0=No, 1=Yes)", y = "Count")

Key Observations: 1. Analyze the balance between enrolled and non-enrolled students. 2. Explore relationships between predictors (e.g., Student Type, Geographic Region) and the Enrolled outcome.

2.3 EDA 2.0

AR Note: Will clean this up to fit in better.

3 Data Transformation

4 Modeling

4.1 Data Splitting

The dataset is split into training (65%) and testing (35%) sets, potentially stratified by Enrolled status or another key categorical variable like Student Type.

set.seed(427)
# Code for splitting Enrolled_FallData into training and testing sets
# Example:
# data_split <- initial_split(Enrolled_FallData, prop = 0.65, strata = Enrolled)
# train_data <- training(data_split)
# test_data  <- testing(data_split)

4.2 Recipe Definitions

Multiple preprocessing recipes are defined for models suitable for binary classification (e.g., logistic regression, random forest, KNN). Recipes might include steps like imputation, dummy variable creation, normalization, and handling skewed predictors.

# Define recipes for preprocessing train_data
# Example recipe for logistic regression:
# base_recipe <- recipe(Enrolled ~ Sex + Student.Type + School..1.Verified.GPA + ..., data = train_data) |>
#   step_impute_median(all_numeric_predictors()) |>
#   step_impute_mode(all_nominal_predictors()) |>
#   step_dummy(all_nominal_predictors()) |>
#   step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors())

# Example recipe for KNN:
# knn_recipe <- base_recipe # Potentially add different imputation or normalization

4.3 Workflow Creation

A workflow_set is created with various model specifications (e.g., logistic regression, KNN with different neighbors) combined with the defined recipes.

# Define model specifications (e.g., logistic_reg(), nearest_neighbor())
# Create a workflow_set combining models and recipes
# Example:
# log_spec <- logistic_reg() |> set_engine("glm")
# knn_spec <- nearest_neighbor(neighbors = tune()) |> set_mode("classification") |> set_engine("kknn")
#
# wf_set <- workflow_set(
#   preproc = list(base = base_recipe, knn = knn_recipe),
#   models = list(log = log_spec, knn = knn_spec)
# )

4.4 Model Evaluation

The models are evaluated using cross-validation on the training set. Metrics appropriate for classification (e.g., Accuracy, ROC AUC, Sensitivity, Specificity) are used to select the best model.

# Perform cross-validation using wf_set and appropriate resampling (e.g., vfold_cv)
# Evaluate performance using metrics like accuracy, roc_auc
# Example:
# cv_folds <- vfold_cv(train_data, v = 10, strata = Enrolled)
# results <- wf_set |>
#   workflow_map("tune_grid", # or "fit_resamples" if no tuning
#                resamples = cv_folds,
#                grid = 10, # Example grid size for tuning KNN
#                metrics = metric_set(accuracy, roc_auc, sensitivity, specificity))
#
# autoplot(results)
# rank_results(results, rank_metric = "roc_auc")

5 Results & Interpretation

5.1 Best Model

Identify the best-performing model based on cross-validation metrics (e.g., the workflow with the highest ROC AUC or desired balance of sensitivity/specificity). For example, a tuned KNN or a logistic regression model might be selected.

5.2 Feature Analysis

Analyze feature importance or coefficients from the best model to understand which factors most strongly predict enrollment. Use techniques like variable importance plots or coefficient plots.

# Fit the best model to the training data and evaluate on the test data
# Extract and visualize feature importance or coefficients
# Example:
# best_workflow <- extract_workflow(results, ...) # Extract the best workflow
# final_fit <- fit(best_workflow, train_data)
# vip_plot <- vip::vip(final_fit) # If applicable
# print(vip_plot)
#
# # Or for logistic regression:
# tidy_fit <- tidy(final_fit)
# print(tidy_fit)

Key Findings: 1. Identify key predictors of enrollment (e.g., High GPA, specific geographic regions, being a transfer student). 2. Quantify the impact of these predictors. 3. Assess model performance on the held-out test set.

6 Limitations & Future Work

6.1 Current Limitations

  1. Data anonymization might obscure important predictive factors.
  2. Missing data in key fields (e.g., test scores, GPA) required imputation.
  3. External factors (e.g., financial aid offers, competing offers) are not included.

6.2 Future Improvements

  1. Incorporate more detailed application data if possible (e.g., essay analysis, recommendation strength).
  2. Include financial aid information.
  3. Develop separate models for freshmen and transfer students if their enrollment drivers differ significantly.
  4. Explore more complex modeling techniques (e.g., gradient boosting, neural networks).

7 Conclusion

The analysis aimed to predict student enrollment using prospect data. The best model achieved [mention best metric, e.g., an ROC AUC of X or Accuracy of Y] on the test set. Key predictors identified include [mention 1-2 key predictors]. While the model provides useful insights, incorporating additional data sources and refining feature engineering could further enhance predictive accuracy and support more targeted admission strategies.x

# Introduction

## Objective

The primary goal of this analysis is to build a predictive model to estimate whether a prospective student will enroll (`Enrolled`) based on their characteristics and interactions with the college. This can help the admissions office optimize recruitment strategies and resource allocation.

## Data Source

The dataset consists of anonymized prospect data for the years 2022-2024, provided by the college's admissions department. Note: If any individual identification is possible, Dr. Friedlander must be contacted immediately.

## Data Description

Initial Prospect Data (`FallData`): 108,437 records. Filtered Prospect Data (`FallData_filtered`): 9,587 records (filtered based on application status). Final Dataset with Enrollment Status (`Enrolled_FallData`): 5,584 records.

The dataset contains records for prospective students. Key features include:

-   **Prospect**: Was information on this prospect purchased? (Y/N)
-   **Inquiry**: Did the student reach out to us first? (Y/N)
-   **Applicant**: Did the student apply? (Y/N)
-   **Admit**: Was the student admitted? (Y/N)
-   **Conversion Rate**: Did the student convert from an admit to a deposit? (Y/N)
-   **Deposit**: Did the student put down a deposit? (Y/N)
-   **Yield Rate**: Did the student convert from a deposit to an enrolled student? (Y/N)
-   **Drops-DP/DF**: Deposits who don’t enroll or defer.
-   **Net Deposits**: Deposits that matriculate that fall.
-   **Sex**: Sex of student.
-   **Student Type**: Freshman or Transfer.
-   **IPEDS Classification**: Race category used by IPEDS.
-   **Geographic Region**: Region where the student lives.
-   **Active County**: County if from Idaho.
-   **First_Source Origin**: Where the prospect data was initially obtained.
-   **First Source Summary**: Summary of the first source.
-   **Application Source**: How the student submitted their application.
-   **Entry Term**: Intended matriculation term.
-   **School X Type**: Type of school X on their application.
-   **School X Institution**: Name of school X on their application.
-   **School X Verified GPA**: GPA from school X.
-   **ACT and SAT variables**: Standardized test scores.
-   **Sport X Sport**: Sport interest.
-   **Academic Interest**: Stated academic interest.
-   **Enrolled**: Target variable created during data cleaning, indicating if the student ultimately enrolled (1 for enrolled, 0 for not enrolled).

The primary task involves cleaning, augmenting, and feature engineering this data to predict the `Enrolled` status based on predictors like `Sex`, `Student Type`, `IPEDS Classification`, `Geographic Region`, `GPA`, test scores, and academic/sport interests.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Data Preparation

## Loading & Cleaning the Data

The data are loaded from `Fall2022Data.csv`, `Fall2023Data.csv`, `Fall2024Data.csv`. Unnecessary features such as thumbnail details, creation details, and others are removed to simplify the dataset.

```{r}
Fall2022Data <- read.csv("Fall2022Data.csv")
Fall2023Data <- read.csv("Fall2023Data.csv")
Fall2024Data <- read.csv("Fall2024Data.csv")

Fall2022Data <- Fall2022Data |>
  mutate(origin = "Fall2022Data")

Fall2023Data <- Fall2023Data |>
  mutate(origin = "Fall2023Data")

Fall2024Data <- Fall2024Data |>
  mutate(origin = "Fall2024Data")

FallData <- bind_rows(Fall2022Data, Fall2023Data, Fall2024Data)

FallData <- FallData |>
  mutate(across(where(is.character), as.factor))


FallData$School..1.Verified.GPA <- ifelse(FallData$School..1.Verified.GPA > 5,
                                          FallData$School..1.Verified.GPA / 100 * 4,
                                          FallData$School..1.Verified.GPA)

FallData$School..2.Verified.GPA <- ifelse(FallData$School..2.Verified.GPA > 5,
                                          FallData$School..2.Verified.GPA / 100 * 4,
                                          FallData$School..2.Verified.GPA)

FallData$School..3.Verified.GPA <- ifelse(
  FallData$School..3.Verified.GPA == 4317,
  FallData$School..3.Verified.GPA / 1000,
  FallData$School..3.Verified.GPA)

FallData$School..3.Verified.GPA <- ifelse(FallData$School..3.Verified.GPA > 5,
                                          FallData$School..3.Verified.GPA / 100 * 4,
                                          FallData$School..3.Verified.GPA)

```

```{r}
# Find the index of the Applicant column
applicant_idx <- which(names(FallData) == "Applicant")

# Filter rows where "Y" appears in Applicant or any column to the right
FallData_filtered <- FallData |>
  filter(apply(
    FallData[, applicant_idx:ncol(FallData)], 1,
    function(row) any(row == "Y")
  ))

admit_idx <- which(names(FallData_filtered) == "Yield.Rate")

Enrolled_FallData <- FallData_filtered |>
  mutate(
    Enrolled = apply(
      FallData_filtered, 1, function(row) {
        # Check if any "Y" in Yield Rate or right of Yield Rate
        any_right = any(row[(admit_idx):(ncol(FallData_filtered))] == "Y", na.rm = TRUE)
        # Check if any "Y" in left of Yield Rate
        any_left = any(row[1:(admit_idx-1)] == "Y", na.rm = TRUE)
        if (isTRUE(any_right)) {
          return(1)
        } else if (isTRUE(any_left)) {
          return(0)
        } else {
          return(NA)
        }
      }
    )
  )

head(Enrolled_FallData)

```

## Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)

We calculate what percentage of total prospects make it to each stage in the admissions funnel: from Prospect → Applicant → Admit → Deposit → Enrolled. This provides a snapshot of how many students move forward at each stage and where the most significant drop-offs occur.

```{r}
# Total number of students
total_students <- nrow(FallData)

# Funnel stage counts
funnel_counts <- c(
  Prospect = sum(FallData$Prospect == "Y", na.rm = TRUE),
  Applicant = nrow(FallData_filtered),
  Admit = sum(FallData$Admit == "Y", na.rm = TRUE),
  Deposit = sum(FallData$Deposit == "Y", na.rm = TRUE),
  Enrolled = sum(Enrolled_FallData$Enrolled == 1, na.rm = TRUE)
)

# Funnel stage percentages
funnel_percentages <- round((funnel_counts / total_students) * 100, 2)

# summary table
funnel_summary <- data.frame(
  Count = funnel_counts,
  Percentage = paste0(funnel_percentages, "%")
)

kable(funnel_summary, caption = "Funnel Stage Conversion Rates (Counts & %)", align = "c")

```

The funnel stage analysis revealed significant drop-offs at each step of the admissions process. Out of 108,437 total records, only 48.55% were identified as prospects, and just 8.84% applied. From there, 5.88% were admitted, 1.00% deposited, and 5.15% ultimately enrolled. The largest drop occurred between prospects and applicants, followed by admitted students who did not deposit. Interestingly, more students were marked as enrolled than deposited, suggesting either data inconsistencies or alternative enrollment pathways. These patterns highlight key areas for improving outreach, engagement, and data tracking across the admissions funnel.

We then created an `Enrolled` variable by scanning for a "Y" response in the `Yield Rate` column or beyond. This helped us classify students who ultimately enrolled.

```{r}
# Finding the index of the Yield Rate column
admit_idx <- which(names(FallData) == "Yield.Rate")

# Created Enrolled column
FallData <- FallData |> 
  mutate(
    Enrolled = apply(FallData, 1, function(row) {
      any_right = any(row[(admit_idx):(ncol(FallData))] == "Y", na.rm = TRUE)
      any_left = any(row[1:(admit_idx - 1)] == "Y", na.rm = TRUE)
      if (isTRUE(any_right)) {
        return(1)
      } else if (isTRUE(any_left)) {
        return(0)
      } else {
        return(NA)
      }
    })
  )

```

### Enrollment by Sex

We examined how enrollment rates vary between male and female students. Female students had a slightly higher enrollment rate than males, while students with unknown sex had a significantly lower enrollment rate.

```{r}
FallData |> 
  mutate(Enrolled = factor(Enrolled)) |> 
  group_by(Sex, Enrolled) |> 
  summarise(count = n()) |> 
  pivot_wider(names_from = Enrolled, values_from = count, values_fill = 0) |> 
  mutate(total = `0` + `1`, enrolled_percent = round(`1` / total * 100, 2))

```

```{r}
ggplot(FallData, aes(x = Sex, fill = factor(Enrolled))) +
  geom_bar(position = "fill") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  labs(title = "Enrollment Rate by Sex", y = "Percentage", fill = "Enrolled") +
  theme_minimal()

```

### Enrollment by Student Type

We compared enrollment between **Freshman** and **Transfer** students. Although the majority of prospects were Freshmen, **Transfer students enrolled at a much higher rate** (over 56%).

```{r}
FallData |> 
  mutate(Enrolled = factor(Enrolled)) |> 
  group_by(`Student.Type`, Enrolled) |> 
  summarise(count = n()) |> 
  pivot_wider(names_from = Enrolled, values_from = count, values_fill = 0) |> 
  mutate(total = `0` + `1`, enrolled_percent = round(`1` / total * 100, 2))

```

```{r}
ggplot(FallData, aes(x = Student.Type, fill = factor(Enrolled))) +
  geom_bar(position = "fill") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  labs(title = "Enrollment Rate by Student Type", y = "Percentage", fill = "Enrolled") +
  theme_minimal()

```

### Enrollment by Sport Interest

We analyzed how primary sport interest impacted enrollment. After grouping and ranking the data, we plotted the **top 10 sports** by number of enrolled students. Some sports had enrollment rates as high as 35–38%, making them promising recruitment areas.

```{r}
FallData |> 
  mutate(Enrolled = factor(Enrolled)) |> 
  group_by(`Sport.1.Sport`, Enrolled) |> 
  summarise(count = n()) |> 
  pivot_wider(names_from = Enrolled, values_from = count, values_fill = 0) |> 
  mutate(total = `0` + `1`, enrolled_percent = round(`1` / total * 100, 2)) |> 
  arrange(desc(`1`))

```

```{r}
sport_plot_data <- FallData |> 
  mutate(Enrolled = factor(Enrolled)) |> 
  group_by(`Sport.1.Sport`, Enrolled) |> 
  summarise(count = n(), .groups = "drop") |> 
  pivot_wider(names_from = Enrolled, values_from = count, values_fill = 0) |> 
  mutate(
    total = `0` + `1`,
    enrolled_percent = round(`1` / total * 100, 2)
  ) |> 
  arrange(desc(`1`)) |> 
  filter(!is.na(`Sport.1.Sport`)) |> 
  slice_max(`1`, n = 10)

# Plot
ggplot(sport_plot_data, aes(x = reorder(`Sport.1.Sport`, enrolled_percent), y = enrolled_percent)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#0073C2FF") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Enrollment Rate by Sport Interest (Top 10)",
    x = "Sport Interest",
    y = "Enrollment Rate (%)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()


```

### Enrollment by Academic Interest

We evaluated how enrollment varied by academic interest. Majors such as **Exercise Science**, **Digital Marketing**, and **Undecided** had notably high enrollment rates. This analysis can help target programs with strong follow-through.

```{r}
FallData |> 
  mutate(Enrolled = factor(Enrolled)) |> 
  group_by(`Academic.Interest`, Enrolled) |> 
  summarise(count = n()) |> 
  pivot_wider(names_from = Enrolled, values_from = count, values_fill = 0) |> 
  mutate(total = `0` + `1`, enrolled_percent = round(`1` / total * 100, 2)) |> 
  arrange(desc(`1`))

```

```{r}
FallData |> 
  filter(!is.na(Academic.Interest)) |> 
  count(Academic.Interest, Enrolled) |> 
  group_by(Academic.Interest) |> 
  mutate(prop = n / sum(n)) |> 
  ungroup() |> 
  group_by(Academic.Interest) |> 
  mutate(total = sum(n)) |> 
  filter(total > 100) |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(Academic.Interest, -prop), y = prop, fill = factor(Enrolled))) +
  geom_col(position = "fill") +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  labs(title = "Enrollment Rate by Academic Interest (Top Majors)", y = "Enrollment %", x = NULL, fill = "Enrolled") +
  theme_minimal()

```

We investigated the **initial lead source** for each student using `First_Source.Origin.First.Source.Summary`. This revealed which outreach methods or partnerships were most effective.

Top lead sources included:

-   Royal Search Responder

-   College Board PSAT, AP, SAT

-   Encoura (ACT)

-   Apply Idaho

We calculated and visualized the enrollment rate for each source (with at least 50 students), revealing which first sources had the **highest yield**.

```{r}
FallData |> 
  count(`First_Source.Origin.First.Source.Summary`, sort = TRUE) |> 
  head(10)

```

```{r}
FallData |> 
  filter(!is.na(`First_Source.Origin.First.Source.Summary`)) |> 
  group_by(`First_Source.Origin.First.Source.Summary`) |> 
  summarise(
    total = n(),
    enrolled = sum(Enrolled == 1, na.rm = TRUE),
    enrolled_rate = round(enrolled / total * 100, 2)
  ) |> 
  filter(total >= 50) |> 
  arrange(desc(enrolled_rate)) |> 
  slice_max(enrolled, n = 10) |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(`First_Source.Origin.First.Source.Summary`, enrolled_rate), y = enrolled_rate)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#0073C2FF") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Enrollment Rate by First Source (Top 10)", y = "Enrollment Rate (%)", x = "First Source") +
  theme_minimal()

```

### Enrollment Distribution

Visualizing the distribution of the `Enrolled` variable:

```{r initial-viz}
# Code to visualize the distribution of the 'Enrolled' variable
# Example:
# ggplot(Enrolled_FallData, aes(x = factor(Enrolled))) +
#  geom_bar() +
#  labs(title = "Distribution of Enrollment Status", x = "Enrolled (0=No, 1=Yes)", y = "Count")

```

**Key Observations:** 1. Analyze the balance between enrolled and non-enrolled students. 2. Explore relationships between predictors (e.g., `Student Type`, `Geographic Region`) and the `Enrolled` outcome.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## EDA 2.0

AR Note: Will clean this up to fit in better.

```{r, eval=FALSE, include=FALSE}
FallDataEDA <- FallData |>
  filter(Admit == "Y")
```

```{r, eval=FALSE, include=FALSE}
FallDataEDA <- FallDataEDA |>
  mutate(
    Sex = if_else(is.na(Sex), "Not Applicable", Sex),
    Student.Type = if_else(is.na(Student.Type), "Not Applicable", Student.Type),
    Academic.Interest = if_else(is.na(Academic.Interest), "Not Applicable", Academic.Interest),
    Application.Source = if_else(is.na(Application.Source), "Not Applicable", Application.Source),
    Geographic.Region = if_else(is.na(Geographic.Region), "Not Applicable", Geographic.Region),
    IPEDS.Classification = if_else(is.na(IPEDS.Classification), "Not Applicable", IPEDS.Classification)
  )
```

```{r, eval=FALSE, include=FALSE}
# Shouldn't the categorical variables be factored?
# I did this separately in the interim to not mess with stuff, but...
FallDataEDA <- FallDataEDA |>
  mutate(
    Sex = as.factor(Sex),
    Student.Type = as.factor(Student.Type),
    Entry.Term = as.factor(Entry.Term),
    Enrolled = as.factor(Enrolled),
    Academic.Interest = as.factor(Academic.Interest),
    Application.Source = as.factor(Application.Source),
    Geographic.Region = as.factor(Geographic.Region),
    IPEDS.Classification = as.factor(IPEDS.Classification)
  )
```

```{r, eval=FALSE, include=FALSE}
ggplot(FallDataEDA, aes(x = Student.Type, fill = Enrolled)) + 
        geom_bar(position = "fill") + 
        labs(
          title = paste("Relationship between student type \nand enrollment status in admitted students"),
          x = "Student Type",
          y = "Enrollment Status (Proportions)") +
        theme_minimal() +
  scale_fill_viridis_d()
```

```{r, eval=FALSE, include=FALSE}
ggplot(FallDataEDA, aes(x = Sex, fill = Enrolled)) + 
        geom_bar(position = "fill") + 
        labs(
          title = paste("Relationship between sex \nand enrollment status in admitted students"),
          x = "Sex",
          y = "Enrollment Status (Proportions)") +
        theme_minimal() +
  scale_fill_viridis_d()
```

```{r, eval=FALSE, include=FALSE}
ggplot(FallDataEDA, aes(x = Entry.Term, fill = Enrolled)) + 
        geom_bar(position = "fill") + 
        labs(
          title = paste("Relationship between entry term \nand enrollment status in admitted students"),
          x = "Entry Term",
          y = "Enrollment Status (Proportions)") +
        theme_minimal() +
  scale_fill_viridis_d() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))
```

```{r, eval=FALSE, include=FALSE}
ggplot(FallDataEDA, aes(x = Academic.Interest, fill = Enrolled)) + 
        geom_bar(position = "fill") + 
        labs(
          title = paste("Relationship between academic interest \nand enrollment status in admitted students"),
          x = "Academic Interest",
          y = "Enrollment Status (Proportions)") +
        theme_minimal() +
  scale_fill_viridis_d() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, size = 7))
```

```{r, eval=FALSE, include=FALSE}
ggplot(FallDataEDA, aes(x = Application.Source, fill = Enrolled)) + 
        geom_bar(position = "fill") + 
        labs(
          title = paste("Relationship between application source \nand enrollment status in admitted students"),
          x = "Application Source",
          y = "Enrollment Status (Proportions)") +
        theme_minimal() +
  scale_fill_viridis_d() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))
```

```{r, eval=FALSE, include=FALSE}
ggplot(FallDataEDA, aes(x = Geographic.Region, fill = Enrolled)) + 
        geom_bar(position = "fill") + 
        labs(
          title = paste("Relationship between geographic region \nand enrollment status in admitted students"),
          x = "Geographic Region",
          y = "Enrollment Status (Proportions)") +
        theme_minimal() +
  scale_fill_viridis_d() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, size = 7))
```

```{r, eval=FALSE, include=FALSE}
ggplot(FallDataEDA, aes(x = IPEDS.Classification, fill = Enrolled)) + 
        geom_bar(position = "fill") + 
        labs(
          title = paste("Relationship between classification \nand enrollment status in admitted students"),
          x = "Classification",
          y = "Enrollment Status (Proportions)") +
        theme_minimal() +
  scale_fill_viridis_d() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, size = 7))
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Data Transformation

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Modeling

## Data Splitting

The dataset is split into training (65%) and testing (35%) sets, potentially stratified by `Enrolled` status or another key categorical variable like `Student Type`.

```{r}
set.seed(427)
# Code for splitting Enrolled_FallData into training and testing sets
# Example:
# data_split <- initial_split(Enrolled_FallData, prop = 0.65, strata = Enrolled)
# train_data <- training(data_split)
# test_data  <- testing(data_split)

```

## Recipe Definitions

Multiple preprocessing recipes are defined for models suitable for binary classification (e.g., logistic regression, random forest, KNN). Recipes might include steps like imputation, dummy variable creation, normalization, and handling skewed predictors.

```{r cache=TRUE}
# Define recipes for preprocessing train_data
# Example recipe for logistic regression:
# base_recipe <- recipe(Enrolled ~ Sex + Student.Type + School..1.Verified.GPA + ..., data = train_data) |>
#   step_impute_median(all_numeric_predictors()) |>
#   step_impute_mode(all_nominal_predictors()) |>
#   step_dummy(all_nominal_predictors()) |>
#   step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors())

# Example recipe for KNN:
# knn_recipe <- base_recipe # Potentially add different imputation or normalization

```

## Workflow Creation

A `workflow_set` is created with various model specifications (e.g., logistic regression, KNN with different neighbors) combined with the defined recipes.

```{r}
# Define model specifications (e.g., logistic_reg(), nearest_neighbor())
# Create a workflow_set combining models and recipes
# Example:
# log_spec <- logistic_reg() |> set_engine("glm")
# knn_spec <- nearest_neighbor(neighbors = tune()) |> set_mode("classification") |> set_engine("kknn")
#
# wf_set <- workflow_set(
#   preproc = list(base = base_recipe, knn = knn_recipe),
#   models = list(log = log_spec, knn = knn_spec)
# )

```

## Model Evaluation

The models are evaluated using cross-validation on the training set. Metrics appropriate for classification (e.g., Accuracy, ROC AUC, Sensitivity, Specificity) are used to select the best model.

```{r cache=TRUE}
# Perform cross-validation using wf_set and appropriate resampling (e.g., vfold_cv)
# Evaluate performance using metrics like accuracy, roc_auc
# Example:
# cv_folds <- vfold_cv(train_data, v = 10, strata = Enrolled)
# results <- wf_set |>
#   workflow_map("tune_grid", # or "fit_resamples" if no tuning
#                resamples = cv_folds,
#                grid = 10, # Example grid size for tuning KNN
#                metrics = metric_set(accuracy, roc_auc, sensitivity, specificity))
#
# autoplot(results)
# rank_results(results, rank_metric = "roc_auc")

```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Results & Interpretation

## Best Model

Identify the best-performing model based on cross-validation metrics (e.g., the workflow with the highest ROC AUC or desired balance of sensitivity/specificity). For example, a tuned KNN or a logistic regression model might be selected.

## Feature Analysis

Analyze feature importance or coefficients from the best model to understand which factors most strongly predict enrollment. Use techniques like variable importance plots or coefficient plots.

```{r model-interpretation}
# Fit the best model to the training data and evaluate on the test data
# Extract and visualize feature importance or coefficients
# Example:
# best_workflow <- extract_workflow(results, ...) # Extract the best workflow
# final_fit <- fit(best_workflow, train_data)
# vip_plot <- vip::vip(final_fit) # If applicable
# print(vip_plot)
#
# # Or for logistic regression:
# tidy_fit <- tidy(final_fit)
# print(tidy_fit)

```

**Key Findings:** 1. Identify key predictors of enrollment (e.g., High GPA, specific geographic regions, being a transfer student). 2. Quantify the impact of these predictors. 3. Assess model performance on the held-out test set.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Limitations & Future Work

## Current Limitations

1.  Data anonymization might obscure important predictive factors.
2.  Missing data in key fields (e.g., test scores, GPA) required imputation.
3.  External factors (e.g., financial aid offers, competing offers) are not included.

## Future Improvements

1.  Incorporate more detailed application data if possible (e.g., essay analysis, recommendation strength).
2.  Include financial aid information.
3.  Develop separate models for freshmen and transfer students if their enrollment drivers differ significantly.
4.  Explore more complex modeling techniques (e.g., gradient boosting, neural networks).

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Conclusion

The analysis aimed to predict student enrollment using prospect data. The best model achieved \[mention best metric, e.g., an ROC AUC of X or Accuracy of Y\] on the test set. Key predictors identified include \[mention 1-2 key predictors\]. While the model provides useful insights, incorporating additional data sources and refining feature engineering could further enhance predictive accuracy and support more targeted admission strategies.x
